architecture.summary: This is a theoretical and survey paper synthesizing mechanistic interpretability findings within a philosophical framework for understanding in LLMs. It does not propose a new system or mechanism, but rather integrates existing MI research (feature detection, world-state tracking, circuit discovery) into a three-tier conceptual hierarchy. Information flows conceptually across levels of computational organization rather than through a specific trained or deployed model.

biblio.title: Mechanistic indicators of understanding in large language models

claims.statement: Conceptual understanding emerges when a model forms 'features' as directions in latent space, learning connections between diverse manifestations of a single entity or property
State-of-the-world understanding emerges when a model learns contingent factual connections between features and dynamically tracks changes in the world
Principled understanding emerges when a model ceases to rely on memorized facts and discovers a compact 'circuit' connecting these facts
Recent findings in mechanistic interpretability render the picture of LLMs as merely imitating linguistic patterns without genuine understanding increasingly untenable
Internal organizations uncovered by MI can underwrite understanding-like unification, but these diverge from human cognition in their parallel exploitation of heterogeneous mechanisms
